key_insight: The paper replaces discrete topological constraints with differentiable Hodge-spectral surrogates that encode homological information through low-frequency Laplacian modes.
engineering_relevance: This enables gradient-based optimization of point clouds and graph complexes with topology-aware losses that are smoother and easier to train than persistent-homology objectives.
method: Builds soft graph and soft clique-complex Hodge-Laplacian relaxations that converge to the ordinary Hodge Laplacian in the hard limit.
Uses low-pass spectral objectives such as heat filters, resolvent filters, and polynomial Laplacian moments to target zero and near-zero modes.
Applies the framework to point clouds and graph clique complexes for topology-constrained optimization.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that the proposed losses produce more spatially distributed gradients, smoother scale-normalized behavior under persistence-pairing changes, and geometry-aware update directions, with graph experiments showing control of normalized first-Betti-type quantities.
watch_item: The approach relies on spectral relaxations and penalty regularization, so surrogate accuracy and tuning may depend on how well the soft operator matches the true topology in the active subcomplex.
paper_title: Hodge Spectral Surrogates for Topology-Constrained Optimization
